title: Collective dynamics of active matter with orientation-weighted alignment
abstract: We study an agent-based model of self-propelled particles with a velocity-dependent alignment rule. This interaction is orientation weighted and acts along the line connecting neighboring particles. Tuning the alignment strength produces several distinct collective regimes, including disordered gas-like motion, coherent flocking, jammed high-density states, and densely ordered moving clusters with active-crystal-like behavior. These results show that a simple local alignment rule can generate a broad range of nonequilibrium collective dynamics within a single microscopic model.
summary: This paper presents a microscopic model of active matter where alignment depends on the orientation of neighboring particles. It shows that this simple rule can lead to complex behaviors ranging from disordered gases to highly ordered, oscillating clusters.
main_problem: Investigating how a simple, local, orientation-weighted alignment rule can generate a diverse range of nonequilibrium collective dynamics, such as flocking, jamming, and clustering.
main_result: The model demonstrates that a single microscopic rule can produce a broad spectrum of states, including coherent flocking, active gases, and dense ordered clusters that exhibit periodic collective reversals.
method: Direct numerical simulations of an agent-based model using the velocity Verlet integration algorithm.
model_or_system: An agent-based model of N self-propelled particles in a 2D arena, featuring a Lennard-Jones-type potential for short-range interactions and a velocity-dependent, orientation-weighted alignment rule.
key_observables: Polar order parameter, Mean Squared Displacement (MSD), sixfold orientational order parameter, and orientational correlation function.
important_parameters: Alignment strength (A), target strength (C), Lennard-Jones interaction strength (Up), and maximum speed (vmax).
main_assumptions: The alignment rule is local and anisotropic; speed is regulated by a saturation rule to prevent unbounded acceleration.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the interaction potentials and the force field of a single agent; Fig 2 illustrates speed truncation and velocity rescaling; Fig 3 compares flocking and active gas regimes; Fig 4 shows system evolution after target removal; Fig 5 displays structural order parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the microscopic alignment rule and model components, explores different dynamical regimes by tuning alignment strength, investigates the effect of localized target forcing, and tests the robustness of these states against noise and perturbations.
why_interesting: The study explores how local, anisotropic, and velocity-dependent rules drive phase transitions and emergent collective behaviors, which is highly relevant to understanding non-equilibrium phase transitions and driven many-body dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Lennard-Jones-type pair potential associated with a central agent (green triangle). The red-shaded region corre- sponds to short-range repulsion that prevents collisions, while the green-shaded region indicates the attractive part of the interaction. (b) Alignment force field generated by a single agent located at the center (green triangle). Arrows show the force direction, and the background color represents the absolute value of the force. Panels (c) and (d) illustrate the dynamics of a two-agent system with the same initial condi- tions, shown by empty red and blue triangles, but opposite signs of the alignment parameter A; the corresponding trajec- tories are shown by solid...
FIG. 2. (a) Speed truncation function f : vi 7→f(vi). (b) Schematics of velocity rescaling, discussed in Section III: speed truncation and rotation by angle ∆θ. ˆvi = vi/|vi|.

FIG. 3. The fragments of the evolution of the system in two different regimes in the absence of external potentials. Agents are depicted with red triangles, the colored lines represent a fragment of their trajectories. (a) Flocking regime A > 0. (b) Active gas A < 0. Detailed simulations can be found in Supplemental Material.

FIG. 4. Representative dynamical protocol for the orientation-weighted active-particle model. The initial condition consists of randomly distributed agents with random velocity directions and speeds. Initially, the system is in the gas-like regime with A = −2. Panels (a) and (b) show the time dependence of the mean squared displacement and polar order parameter, respectively. At t = −50, the anisotropic target field [Eq. (9)] is switched on, and the alignment strength is changed to A = 2. The agents then condense into a dense ordered cluster near the target. At t = 0, the parameters A and C are changed again, producing the post-target regimes shown in panel (c): flocking, active gas,...

FIG. 5. Local sixfold order and orientational correlation func- tion for the dense ordered state. Panels (a,c) correspond to the unperturbed state shown in Fig. 4 at t = 0, while panels (b,d) show the state obtained with the stochastic- friction perturbation of Eq. (14).
